# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE WITHOUT DIRECT OWNER APPROVAL

## What This File Is

This file is the phase-1 consolidation record for the correction-loop spec work.

Its purpose is to:

1. record owner-made decisions as primary chunks of source material;
2. preserve those chunks in a traceable and reversible form;
3. accept later approved section text as amendments to matching chunks without overwriting the original chunk text;
4. stage duplicate, overlapping, and conflicting intent for later reconciliation without rewriting approved content in place.

This file is not the final polished spec. It is the protected working record of what is known.

## File Rules

- Primary chunk text records owner decisions and must not be silently rewritten.
- Approved section text, when added later, must be attached as an amendment to the matching chunk or added as new material if no matching chunk exists.
- Amendments do not replace the primary chunk text in this file unless the owner explicitly approves that replacement.
- Inventory notes at the bottom may identify duplicate, overlapping, or conflicting intent, but they do not change chunk content by themselves.
- Once this file is considered complete, it is locked and forbidden from change without direct owner approval.

---

## Primary Chunks

### CH-01 — Spec Authority During Finalization

**Primary chunk**

Once the spec is written, the spec is the authority. Earlier handoffs and notes may inform review, question generation, and verification, but they do not become the governing authority inside the spec itself.

**Amendment 01 — Approved section text from this conversation**

> ## 1. The governing standards for this work
>
> This spec, once approved, is the design of record for the correction loop. During this final review pass, the spec text is checked against three reference sources, each with a distinct role:
>
> 1. **The owner's stated constraints (verification standard during review).** The 2026-05-17 handoff records the owner's USER EDIT answers given during the design conversation that produced this spec; further owner decisions are recorded in the 2026-05-20 review session log. Together these are the standard against which the spec is verified during this review. If the spec text drifts from an owner constraint, the spec is corrected; the constraint is not reframed. After this review closes, the spec stands as the authority; the owner constraints remain as historical evidence of what the design must encode.
>
> 2. **Surviving invariants from the 2026-05-12 plan and contract (technical constraints).** Specific items not under amendment by this rework — primarily contract line 197's declared-input discipline, the spec/architecture separation, and the existing pipeline boundary contracts for stages this rework does not touch — are governing technical constraints. Items in the plan or contract that are under amendment by this rework cannot self-govern their own amendment and are not governing input here. Process discipline (FAILED-handoff methodology, no-reactive-overshoot, surface-contradictions, design-before-implementation, no-grading-findings, review-not-verify) lives in a separate agent-facing operating reference; it does not appear inside this spec as design content.
>
> 3. **The Expert Standard skill (evaluation frame).** The frame for engineering judgment is: evaluate against engineering standards, not against existing code patterns. Concretely for this design, the chosen working axes are determinism, auditability, interface honesty, and operability; every design decision is tested against both failure poles (over-rigid hard gate and under-defined run-time improvisation) before being accepted. These specific working axes and the both-poles test are this spec's chosen concrete evaluation method, not language mandated by the Expert Standard skill itself.
>
> **What is in force from the owner's stated constraints:**
>
> a. Corrections have multiple origins — the design-reviewer catching a defect, the owner directing a change, or a failure elsewhere in the pipeline that source-traces back to architecture. (USER EDIT 2026-05-17 line 15.)
>
> b. The origin of a problem is determined in real time, not by a fixed pre-decided mapping of problem-type to action-stage — a failed plan can trace to the architecture, the spec, or something else; the actual source is found in the moment. (USER EDIT 2026-05-17 line 21.)
>
> c. The opt-in pause is implemented at the `/architecture` orchestration layer. The AgentBoard app's blocking-gate mechanism is a separate existing feature, not modified by this work and not depended on. (USER EDIT 2026-05-17 line 23.)
>
> d. No hard gate forces (i) the spec being immutable, (ii) a fixed retry cap that simply stops and hands the problem to the owner without investigation, or (iii) any other fixed behavior where the situation requires in-the-moment determination. (USER EDIT 2026-05-17 lines 19/20/21.)
>
> e. The architecture correction flow does not directly edit the spec. The spec can be modified when source-trace finds the origin within it. The modification path is not `/foundation` (which is for spec creation only) and is otherwise handled outside this spec; its specifics are out of scope here. (Owner [DECISION] 2026-05-20.)
>
> f. Agents investigate the origin of repeated failure themselves; escalation to the owner happens specifically when the trace lands on a spec-origin determination. The investigation function lives in an external investigator agent that is out of scope for this spec; its capabilities and behavior are not asserted here. This spec acknowledges the investigator as an external dependency that receives the handoff at the cap. (USER EDIT 2026-05-17 line 20; owner [DECISION] 2026-05-20.)
>
> g. The correction loop has a finite bound of 3 retries on the same card. On the bound, the external investigator agent runs root cause analysis; this spec hands off and does not itself perform source-trace. (Owner [DECISION] 2026-05-20.)

### CH-02 — Process Discipline Lives Outside The Spec

**Primary chunk**

How agents are supposed to work through the implementation and cleanup process should not be encoded inside the product-design spec as if it were part of the design itself. That process guidance belongs in a separate reference file for agents to use while the remaining changes are being made.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-03 — The Current Failure Has Two Missing Pieces

**Primary chunk**

The current failure is not solved by simply deciding that correction-mode work should happen. The affected stage is missing both:

1. a correction input for receiving correction-mode work; and
2. instructions for how to operate when correction-mode work is present.

Without both of those, the system is asking a creation flow to do rework it was not designed to handle.

**Amendment 01 — Approved section text from this conversation**

> ## 2. The contradiction being resolved
>
> The correction loop must support substantive rework of architecture output at the stage where the problem actually originates. That requires more than deciding that a correction is needed. It requires the affected stage to support correction-mode work explicitly.
>
> The current system does not support that correctly. When substantive corrections are routed back into architecture work, the affected stage has no declared correction input for receiving that work, and its instructions only define the initial create-from-scratch flow. The system can therefore determine that revision is required without providing either the interface or the process needed to carry that revision out properly.
>
> This design resolves that contradiction by requiring both of the following:
>
> 1. The affected stage must declare a correction input that carries substantive correction work explicitly.
> 2. The affected stage must define a correction-mode process that is used when that input is present.
>
> That correction-mode process must be distinct from the initial create-from-scratch flow. It must tell the stage how to interpret correction work, how to revise existing output, and how to operate when the task is rework rather than initial generation.
>
> The current workaround is not the intended design. This spec exists to define the explicit correction path that is currently missing.

**Amendment 02 — Approved section text from this conversation**

> ## 3. What this design is
>
> This design defines an explicit correction path for substantive rework. A correction is carried to the stage that must redo work as a declared, auditable input, not as undeclared prompt context and not as an implicit workaround inside the architecture flow.
>
> Three properties define that path:
>
> - **Multi-origin.** A correction may originate from the design-reviewer catching a defect, from the owner directing a change, or from a failure elsewhere in the pipeline that is source-traced back to architecture.
> - **Routed to the determined origin.** The problem goes back to wherever it actually originated, determined in real time rather than by a fixed pre-decided mapping.
> - **Revision is a first-class mode.** When a stage receives correction work, it must follow an explicit correction-mode process rather than trying to use the initial create-from-scratch flow.
>
> This design exists to make correction-mode work explicit at both the interface level and the process level, so the system no longer depends on workaround behavior inside the architecture flow.

### CH-04 — Existing Inputs Are Not Correction Inputs

**Primary chunk**

The stage already has inputs, but those are not the same thing as a dedicated correction input. Inputs that support the normal create-from-scratch workflow do not, by themselves, define how substantive correction-mode work is received or processed.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-05 — Correction-Mode Needs Its Own Instructions

**Primary chunk**

Everything the affected stage currently knows is a step-by-step process for doing one kind of work. Giving it an incompatible correction task without dedicated correction-mode instructions will not produce results as good as a process that explicitly tells it what to do when correction or rework is happening.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-06 — Architecture Does Not Directly Edit The Spec

**Primary chunk**

The architecture correction flow should not directly edit the spec as an in-flow workaround.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-07 — The Spec Can Be Modified If Investigation Finds The Origin There

**Primary chunk**

If investigation determines that the origin of the issue is within the spec, then the spec can be modified on that basis. The existence of spec-origin failure must not be blocked by a false rule that the spec can never change.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-08 — `/foundation` Is Not The Spec-Modification Channel

**Primary chunk**

`/foundation` is for spec creation, not for spec modification. If a problem traces back to the spec, the path for modifying the spec is not `/foundation`.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-09 — Retry Cap Is 3

**Primary chunk**

The correction loop has a finite retry cap of 3 on the same card.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-10 — Root Cause Analysis Runs After 3 Failures

**Primary chunk**

After 3 failures of the same card, root cause analysis runs.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-11 — Investigation Must Be Separate From Review

**Primary chunk**

The investigation function must be separate from the reviewer's function. If the reviewer is also made responsible for deciding when to stop retrying and start deeper investigation, it can produce corrections lists that include material outside the scope of the stage it is sending work back to.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-12 — Investigator Agent Owns The Root-Cause Handoff

**Primary chunk**

The investigator agent is the separate function that takes over when the retry cap is hit and deeper issue tracing is needed.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-13 — Investigator Agent Is Out Of Scope For This Spec

**Primary chunk**

The investigator agent is already handled and covered elsewhere. It is out of scope for this spec.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._

### CH-14 — Investigator Agent Runs Automatically With External Context Access

**Primary chunk**

The investigator agent runs automatically and has access to chat logs, card information, and other relevant functions needed to perform root cause analysis. This spec may acknowledge that handoff, but it does not define the investigator's internal behavior.

**Amendment**

_No approved section amendment attached yet._
